## [Report Description](#collapse-A)

## Animal Produce Market Outlook

The global animal produce market size was estimated at USD 1.6 trillion in 2023 and is expected to reach USD 2.3 trillion by 2032, reflecting a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 3.9% during the forecast period. This steady growth is fueled by the rising demand for protein-rich diets, increasing consumer preference for organic and natural food products, and technological advancements in animal husbandry and processing. The continuous expansion of the food and beverage industry and the growing population are significant contributors to the market's expansion, as they drive the demand for various animal-derived products across the globe.

One of the primary growth factors for the animal produce market is the rising consumer awareness regarding nutritional intake and health benefits associated with animal products. Dairy, meat, and egg products are rich in essential nutrients like proteins, vitamins, and minerals, making them integral components of balanced diets. As more consumers become health-conscious, there is an increasing demand for high-quality, sustainably sourced animal produce. This trend is further bolstered by the growing number of health and wellness campaigns, which promote the consumption of nutrient-dense foods for optimal health. Additionally, the shifting consumer preference towards organic and grass-fed animal products, which are perceived to be healthier and more environmentally friendly, is also driving market growth.

The development of advanced technologies in animal farming and processing has significantly enhanced the efficiency and quality of animal produce. Innovations such as precision livestock farming, genetic modifications, and automation in processing facilities have improved production yields and reduced operational costs, making animal products more accessible to a broader consumer base. These technological advancements also address concerns related to food safety and quality, as they enable more controlled and hygienic environments for raising and processing animals. As a result, the adoption of such technologies is expected to contribute positively to the growth of the animal produce market during the forecast period.

Another critical factor influencing the market is the increasing demand for sustainable and ethically produced animal products. With growing awareness about the environmental and ethical implications of conventional animal farming methods, consumers are increasingly gravitating towards products that are marketed as sustainable and cruelty-free. This shift is encouraging producers to adopt more eco-friendly and humane practices, such as free-range farming and organic feed, to meet consumer expectations. Furthermore, regulatory bodies are imposing stricter guidelines on animal welfare and environmental impact, prompting producers to innovate and invest in sustainable practices, which in turn is driving market growth.

## Dairy Analysis

The dairy segment is a pivotal component of the animal produce market, driven by its widespread consumption and nutritional value. Dairy products, including milk, cheese, butter, and yogurt, are staple dietary components across various cultures. The segment's growth is propelled by the increasing demand for high-protein diets and the expanding popularity of value-added products, such as flavored yogurts and lactose-free milk variants. Furthermore, the dairy industry benefits from continuous innovation in processing techniques, which enhance shelf-life and improve product quality. The rising trend of health-conscious consumers opting for low-fat and organic dairy products also contributes significantly to the segment's expansion.

Technological advancements in dairy farming and processing are playing a crucial role in boosting productivity and efficiency. Automation in milking processes, genetic enhancements in cattle, and improved feed formulations have led to higher milk yields and better quality produce. Additionally, the implementation of IoT and data analytics in dairy farming is facilitating precise monitoring of livestock health and production, thereby reducing costs and ensuring consistent quality. These advancements are instrumental in meeting the growing global demand for dairy products and are expected to continue driving growth in this segment.

Consumer preferences are increasingly shifting towards organic and natural dairy products, influenced by the perceived health benefits and environmental concerns associated with conventional dairy farming. This trend is encouraging producers to adopt sustainable and organic farming practices, such as pasture-raised cattle and organic feed, to cater to the growing demand. Furthermore, the rise of plant-based dairy alternatives, driven by veganism and lactose intolerance, presents both challenges and opportunities for the traditional dairy market. While some consumers are transitioning to plant-based options, others continue to seek premium dairy products with unique flavors and nutritional benefits.

The dairy segment is also witnessing growth due to its application in various industries beyond food and beverages. In the pharmaceutical sector, dairy-derived ingredients are used in nutritional supplements and therapeutic products, leveraging their bioactive properties. The cosmetics industry is increasingly incorporating dairy components in skincare products, capitalizing on their moisturizing and anti-aging benefits. Additionally, the demand for dairy-based ingredients in functional foods and nutraceuticals is rising, further diversifying the market opportunities for this segment.

## Meat Analysis

The meat segment is one of the most significant components of the animal produce market, driven by its essential role in protein consumption worldwide. With a diverse range of products including beef, pork, poultry, and lamb, the meat industry caters to a wide array of cultural and dietary preferences. The segment's growth is primarily fueled by the increasing demand for protein-rich diets, particularly in developing regions where meat consumption is rising as incomes grow. Moreover, the ongoing development of new meat processing techniques and value-added products, such as ready-to-eat meals and marinated meats, further stimulates market expansion.

Technological innovations in meat production are enhancing efficiency and sustainability, which are critical factors in meeting global demand. Advances in breeding techniques, feed optimization, and waste management are leading to improved meat yields and reduced environmental impact. Furthermore, the adoption of automated processing systems and quality control technologies is ensuring the safety and consistency of meat products. These technological enhancements are crucial for maintaining competitiveness in the global meat market and are expected to drive growth in the coming years.

Consumer preferences in the meat industry are increasingly influenced by concerns related to health, sustainability, and animal welfare. There is a growing trend towards organic and grass-fed meat products, perceived to be healthier and more environmentally friendly than conventionally produced meat. Producers are responding by implementing sustainable farming practices and improving transparency in supply chains to meet consumer expectations. Additionally, the rise of plant-based and cultured meat alternatives presents both challenges and opportunities for the traditional meat market. While some consumers are shifting towards these alternatives, others continue to seek premium and ethically produced meat products.

The meat segment's application extends beyond food into sectors such as pharmaceuticals and pet nutrition. Collagen and gelatin, derived from animal bones and skin, are used extensively in medical and pharmaceutical applications, including wound care and drug delivery systems. In the pet food industry, meat products are essential for formulating balanced and nutritious diets for companion animals. These diverse applications highlight the multifaceted nature of the meat market and the potential for growth across various sectors.

## Eggs Analysis

The eggs segment is a vital part of the animal produce market, driven by its role as a versatile and nutrient-rich food source. Eggs are consumed globally in a variety of forms, from breakfast staples to ingredients in bakery and confectionery products. The segment's growth is fueled by the rising demand for protein-rich diets and the increasing popularity of eggs as a convenient and affordable source of nutrition. Moreover, the development of value-added egg products, such as liquid eggs and egg substitutes, is expanding the market scope and catering to diverse consumer preferences.

Advancements in poultry farming and egg production technologies are enhancing efficiency and productivity, contributing to the segment's growth. Improvements in breeding, feed formulations, and housing systems are leading to higher egg yields and better quality produce. Furthermore, the implementation of automated sorting and packaging systems is optimizing supply chain operations and ensuring product safety. These technological advancements are essential for meeting the growing global demand for eggs and maintaining competitiveness in the market.

Consumer preferences in the egg market are increasingly influenced by considerations related to health, sustainability, and animal welfare. There is a growing trend towards organic and free-range eggs, perceived to be healthier and more ethically produced than conventionally farmed eggs. Producers are responding by adopting sustainable and humane farming practices and enhancing transparency in supply chains to meet consumer expectations. Additionally, the rise of plant-based egg alternatives presents both challenges and opportunities for the traditional egg market. While some consumers are shifting towards these alternatives, others continue to seek premium and ethically produced egg products.

The eggs segment also finds application in various industries beyond food and beverages. In the pharmaceutical sector, egg-derived components are used in vaccines and other medical formulations, leveraging their unique biological properties. The cosmetics industry is incorporating egg-based ingredients in skincare products, capitalizing on their moisturizing and anti-aging benefits. These diverse applications underscore the eggs segment's potential for growth across multiple sectors and highlight the importance of innovation in meeting evolving consumer needs.

## Wool Analysis

The wool segment is a significant component of the animal produce market, driven by its essential role in the textile industry. Wool is highly valued for its natural properties, including insulation, durability, and moisture-wicking capabilities, making it an ideal material for clothing and home textiles. The segment's growth is fueled by the increasing demand for sustainable and eco-friendly textiles, as consumers become more environmentally conscious. Moreover, the development of advanced processing techniques and innovative wool blends is expanding the market scope and catering to diverse consumer preferences.

Technological advancements in wool production are enhancing efficiency and sustainability, contributing to the segment's growth. Improvements in sheep breeding and shearing techniques are leading to higher wool yields and better quality fibers. Furthermore, the implementation of eco-friendly processing methods, such as waterless dyeing and biodegradable treatments, is reducing the environmental impact of wool production. These technological enhancements are essential for maintaining competitiveness in the global wool market and are expected to drive growth in the coming years.

Consumer preferences in the wool market are increasingly influenced by considerations related to sustainability and ethical production. There is a growing trend towards organic and ethically sourced wool, perceived to be more environmentally friendly than conventionally produced wool. Producers are responding by adopting sustainable and humane farming practices and enhancing transparency in supply chains to meet consumer expectations. Additionally, the rise of synthetic and plant-based textile alternatives presents both challenges and opportunities for the traditional wool market. While some consumers are shifting towards these alternatives, others continue to seek premium and ethically produced wool products.

The wool segment's application extends beyond textiles into sectors such as construction and automotive. Wool is used as an eco-friendly insulation material in building projects, capitalizing on its natural properties and sustainability credentials. In the automotive industry, wool is incorporated into interior components, leveraging its durability and aesthetic appeal. These diverse applications highlight the multifaceted nature of the wool market and the potential for growth across various sectors.

## Honey Analysis

The honey segment is an integral part of the animal produce market, driven by its role as a natural sweetener and its numerous health benefits. Honey is consumed globally in a variety of forms, from culinary uses to medicinal applications. The segment's growth is fueled by the rising demand for natural and organic sweeteners, as consumers become more health-conscious. Moreover, the development of value-added honey products, such as flavored and infused variants, is expanding the market scope and catering to diverse consumer preferences.

Advancements in apiculture and honey production technologies are enhancing efficiency and productivity, contributing to the segment's growth. Improvements in bee breeding, hive management, and extraction techniques are leading to higher honey yields and better quality produce. Furthermore, the implementation of automated packaging systems and quality control technologies is optimizing supply chain operations and ensuring product safety. These technological advancements are essential for meeting the growing global demand for honey and maintaining competitiveness in the market.

Consumer preferences in the honey market are increasingly influenced by considerations related to health, sustainability, and ethical production. There is a growing trend towards organic and raw honey, perceived to be healthier and more sustainably produced than processed honey. Producers are responding by adopting sustainable and humane apiculture practices and enhancing transparency in supply chains to meet consumer expectations. Additionally, the rise of plant-based sweeteners presents both challenges and opportunities for the traditional honey market. While some consumers are shifting towards these alternatives, others continue to seek premium and ethically produced honey products.

The honey segment also finds application in various industries beyond food and beverages. In the pharmaceutical sector, honey-derived components are used in wound care and other medical formulations, leveraging their unique antibacterial properties. The cosmetics industry is incorporating honey-based ingredients in skincare products, capitalizing on their moisturizing and anti-inflammatory benefits. These diverse applications underscore the honey segment's potential for growth across multiple sectors and highlight the importance of innovation in meeting evolving consumer needs.

## Opportunities & Threats

The animal produce market presents significant opportunities for growth, driven by the increasing demand for high-quality, sustainably sourced products. As consumers become more health-conscious, there is a growing preference for organic and natural animal products, which are perceived to be healthier and more environmentally friendly. This trend is encouraging producers to adopt sustainable and humane farming practices, such as free-range and grass-fed systems, to meet consumer expectations. Additionally, the rise of e-commerce and online distribution channels is expanding market reach and providing consumers with greater access to a diverse range of animal products. These developments present lucrative opportunities for market players to innovate and cater to evolving consumer preferences.

Technological advancements in animal farming and processing are also creating opportunities for growth in the animal produce market. Innovations such as precision livestock farming, automation, and data analytics are enhancing efficiency and productivity, reducing costs, and ensuring consistent quality. These technologies enable producers to optimize operations, improve product safety, and meet the growing global demand for animal products. Furthermore, the development of value-added products, such as fortified and functional animal-derived ingredients, is expanding the market scope and catering to diverse consumer needs. These advancements present opportunities for market players to differentiate their offerings and gain a competitive edge.

Despite the opportunities, the animal produce market faces several challenges that could hinder growth. One of the primary restrainers is the increasing competition from plant-based and cultured alternatives, which are gaining popularity among health-conscious and environmentally aware consumers. These alternatives present a threat to traditional animal products, as they offer similar nutritional benefits with perceived lower environmental impacts and ethical concerns. Additionally, the market is challenged by regulatory and sustainability pressures, as governments and organizations implement stricter guidelines on animal welfare and environmental impact. Producers must adapt to these changing regulations and invest in sustainable practices to remain competitive and mitigate potential risks.

## Regional Outlook

The animal produce market is characterized by varying regional dynamics, reflecting differences in consumer preferences, cultural practices, and economic development. North America is a prominent market, driven by high consumption levels of dairy, meat, and eggs, and a strong preference for organic and natural products. The region is witnessing a growing demand for sustainable and ethically produced animal products, which is encouraging producers to adopt eco-friendly farming practices. With a CAGR of 3.5%, North America's market is expected to continue its steady growth, supported by technological advancements and the expansion of online distribution channels.

In Europe, the animal produce market is influenced by stringent regulations on animal welfare and environmental sustainability. The region is characterized by a strong preference for organic and grass-fed products, driven by consumer awareness of health and environmental issues. European producers are increasingly focusing on transparency and traceability in supply chains to meet regulatory requirements and consumer expectations. The market's growth is supported by innovations in processing and packaging technologies, which enhance product quality and safety. With a CAGR of 3.2%, Europe is expected to maintain its position as a leading market for premium and sustainable animal products.

The Asia Pacific region is experiencing significant growth in the animal produce market, driven by rising incomes, urbanization, and changing dietary habits. The demand for protein-rich diets is increasing, particularly in countries like China and India, where meat and dairy consumption is on the rise. The region is also witnessing a growing interest in organic and natural products, as health and wellness trends gain traction. With a CAGR of 4.1%, Asia Pacific is expected to be one of the fastest-growing markets, supported by investments in infrastructure and technological advancements in farming and processing. However, challenges related to supply chain logistics and regulatory compliance must be addressed to fully capitalize on this growth potential.

## Consumer Demand For Animal Products In 25 Years

![Favorite](https://faunalytics.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/SAVE_TO_LIBRARY.svg "Favorite")![Loading](https://faunalytics.org/wp-content/plugins/wp-favorite-posts/img/loading.gif "Loading")
![Loading](https://faunalytics.org/wp-content/plugins/wp-favorite-posts/img/loading.gif "Loading")

Global demand for animal-based foods has increased substantially in recent decades as part of the nutrition transition in many countries. This shift has been linked to both positive and negative effects on human health and the environment. While these foods can provide important nutrients and support livelihoods, their production and consumption have also been associated with increased risks of certain diseases and significant environmental impacts. Given these widespread and multidimensional effects, **it’s crucial to understand how factors like income, prices, and consumer preferences may influence future demand for foods from farmed animals**.

A study conducted by an international team of researchers aimed to predict how demand for ‘livestock-derived’ foods might evolve between 2020 and 2050 under different scenarios. These foods include beef, sheep and goat meat, pork, poultry, dairy milk, and eggs.

The researchers used the International Model for Policy Analysis of Agricultural Commodities and Trade (IMPACT), a global economic model, to simulate various “what-if” scenarios based on different trajectories for population growth, income growth, and changes in consumer preferences.

The IMPACT model incorporated data on population and income growth from three of the Shared Socioeconomic Pathways (SSPs) — narratives used to explore how global society, demographics, and economics might change in the future:

The researchers also examined scenarios where the income elasticity of demand for red meat (a measure of how demand responds to changes in income) declined over time, either globally or just in high-income countries. This allowed them to explore how changes in consumer preferences might affect future demand.

The study projects that **under the middle-range SSP2 scenario, global demand for protein from livestock-derived foods will increase by 14% per person and 38% in total between 2020 and 2050.** The fastest growth in per-person demand is expected in South Asia and sub-Saharan Africa, with increases of 49% and 55% respectively, though starting from lower initial levels than other regions.

**In high-income countries, red meat demand is particularly sensitive to changes in income growth and consumer preferences.** If the income elasticity of demand for red meat in these countries declines by 100% by 2050, per-person demand would decrease by roughly 3% compared to 2020 levels. However, if this decline occurs globally, per-person red meat demand in high-income countries would paradoxically increase by about 9%, due to the effect of lower global prices outweighing reduced income responsiveness.

The research highlights complex interactions between income, prices, and consumer preferences in shaping future demand. For instance, **slower global income growth can reduce demand in some regions, leading to lower global prices that may increase demand in price-sensitive markets**. The study also projects that high-income countries’ share of global red meat protein demand will decrease from 31% in 2020 to 26% in 2050 under the SSP2 scenario.

Additional insights include:

The authors acknowledge some limitations of their study. They didn’t separate out specific products or cuts of meat, for instance, but analyzed commodities like ‘beef’ or ‘pork’ as a whole, yet demand for cuts perceived as low or high quality may very well be different. They also recognize the ongoing need for improved estimation of elasticities.

These findings have important implications for human health, environmental sustainability, and global food systems. Understanding potential trajectories for animal-based food demand can help policymakers, health professionals, and environmental scientists better prepare for and potentially influence future consumption patterns. Although animal advocates may be discouraged by the thought of a future with more meat, eggs, and dairy, **the complex interactions between income, prices, and consumer preferences highlighted in this study underscore the need for nuanced approaches when considering interventions to shape future food demand**.

![](https://faunalytics.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/06/aeneas-headshot.jpg)

Citations:

Komarek, A. M., Dunston, S., Enahoro, D., Godfray, H. C. J., Herrero, M., Mason-D’Croz, D., Rich, K. M., Scarborough, P., Springmann, M., Sulser, T. B., Wiebe, K., & Willenbockel, D. (2021). Income, consumer preferences, and the future of livestock-derived food demand. *Global Environmental Change*, *70*, 102343. [<https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2021.102343>](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gloenvcha.2021.102343)

Addressing the 2050 demand for terrestrial animal source food
=============================================================

[Alison L Van Eenennaam](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Van%20Eenennaam%20AL%22%5BAuthor%5D)

### Alison L Van Eenennaam

a Department of Animal Science, University of California, Davis, CA 95616

Find articles by [Alison L Van Eenennaam](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=%22Van%20Eenennaam%20AL%22%5BAuthor%5D)

a,1

*   Author information
*   Article notes
*   Copyright and License information

a Department of Animal Science, University of California, Davis, CA 95616

1
Email: alvaneenennaam@ucdavis.edu.

Edited by Rodolphe Barrangou, North Carolina State University, Raleigh, NC; received January 20, 2024; accepted November 5, 2024

### Series information

Sustainability of Animal-Sourced Foods and Plant-Based Alternatives

Issue date 2024 Dec 10.

Copyright © 2024 the Author(s). Published by PNAS.

This open access article is distributed under [Creative Commons Attribution License 4.0 (CC BY)](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/).

[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)

PMCID: PMC11648654 PMID: [39621917](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39621917/)

Abstract
--------

The high emissions intensity of terrestrial animal source food (TASF) and projected increasing demand in low- and middle-income countries (LMIC) have spurred interest in the development of animal-free alternatives and manufactured food items that aim to substitute for meat, milk, and eggs with the promise of reduced environmental impact of producing food. The developing world is the source of 75% of global emissions from ruminants and will house 86% of the world’s human population by 2050. The adoption of cost-effective, genetic, feed and nutrition practices, and improving livestock health in LMIC are seen as the most promising interventions to reduce emissions resulting from projected increased TASF demand though 2050. Genetic improvement is a particularly attractive approach to productivity enhancements, as such improvements are permanent and cumulative. Alternative proteins may play a role in addressing demand for affordable sources of nutrient-dense foods, however, price will be a major factor influencing adoption given 3.1 billion people globally (42%) were currently unable to afford a healthy diet in 2021. Additionally, there is currently a mismatch between the location of alternative protein companies, and both projected increased TASF demand and emissions. To date, the vast majority (>81%) of these companies are based in high-income countries. The sustainability implications of replacing TASF with alternative proteins at scale needs to consider not only environmental metrics but also the wider economic and social sustainability impacts, given the essential role that livestock play in the livelihoods and food security of approximately 1.3 billion people.

**Keywords:** livestock, sustainability, cattle

* * *

Terrestrial animal-source foods (TASF) such as meat, milk, and eggs are nutrient dense and support the livelihoods and food security of almost 1.3 billion people, including almost 930 million Africans and South Asians ([1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r1)). However, their production is associated with higher levels of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions as compared to other foods ([2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r2), [3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r3)). A Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) life cycle assessment (LCA) based on the reference year 2015 ([4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r4)) found TASF were associated with emissions of 6.2 gigatonnes (Gt) of carbon dioxide equivalent (CO 2 eq), with over half being contributed by cattle (62%), followed by pigs (14%), chickens (9%), buffaloes (8%), and small ruminants (sheep and goats, 7%), equating to approximately 12% of all anthropogenic GHG emissions. High-income countries have developed efficient livestock production systems, which reduce both the cost and emissions intensity of animal products. However, these improvements have not been widely adopted in developing countries where the majority of livestock are located. It has been estimated that population and demand growth, primarily in Africa where the population is projected to increase by 80 percent by 2050 relative to 2020 levels, will drive a further 20% per capita increase in animal product demand by 2050. In the absence of any intervention or improvements in productivity, it is estimated that this increase will drive global livestock numbers and hence emissions to nearly 9.1 Gt CO 2 eq by 2050 ([5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r5)).

One response to these projections could be to propose a rapid removal of animals from global food systems ([6](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r6)). While this would reduce GHG emissions, consideration of the needs and livelihoods of people in low- and middle-income (LMIC) countries are frequently absent from these discussions ([7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r7)). In many LMIC households, diets are already predominantly plant-based because of the high price of nutrient-dense TASF. The cereal-based diets of the poor lack essential micronutrients, resulting in childhood stunting (low-height-for-age) which affected approximately 26.6% (21.5 to 32.4%) of children under five in 2017, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa and Asia, where stunting rates exceeding 30% still occur ([8](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r8)). Of the 176.1 million children who were stunted in 2017, over half lived in only four countries: India (51.5 million), Pakistan (10.7 million), Nigeria (11.8 million), and China (16.2 million) ([9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r9)). Compared to plant-based foods, TASF supply higher quality protein and important micro- and macronutrients including vitamin A, vitamin B12, vitamin D3, iron, iodine, zinc, calcium, and folic acid ([7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r7)). Rising incomes in LMIC are typically associated with a dietary shift from plant-sourced starchy staples to high-quality animal-sourced proteins from meat, eggs, and dairy, referred to the LMIC protein transition ([10](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r10)).

Previous work hypothetically modeled the nutritional and GHG implications of removing animals from US agriculture. It was found that while this did in fact decrease agricultural GHGs (28%) and total emissions (2.6%), plant-only diets formulated for the US population had an excess of dietary energy and resulted in a greater number of deficiencies in essential nutrients ([11](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r11), [12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r12)). A less extreme approach that has been suggested by many authors is to decrease the intake of TASF, especially red meat consumption across wealthy and upper-middle-income economies ([13](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r13)), where typical diets include high amounts of TASF. This was modeled in the United States by replacing 10% of beef expenditures with other foods, or alternatively 10%, 30%, and 60% of beef expenditures were replaced with plant-based alternatives. This was found to reduce the number of animals needed for beef production by 2 to 12 million and reduced the carbon footprint of US food production by 2.5 to 13.5%. ([14](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r14)). This study did not examine nutritional implications of such a shift.

Behrens et al. ([15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r15)) compared the dietary consumption patterns of average people with the nationally recommended food-based dietary guidelines (FBDG) in high-income and upper-middle-income nations and found FBDGs were associated with reductions in GHG of 13.0 to 24.8%, and 0.8 to 12.2%, respectively. The majority (54%) of the reduced environmental impact in high-income countries was driven by reductions in calories (i.e., eating the recommended number of calories rather than in excess of what is needed), and 46% by shifting to increased fruit, vegetables, and nuts at the expense of sugars, oils, meat, and dairy. Conversely, in LMIC, FBDGs were associated with increases in GHG by 12.4 to 17.0%. This was because of the recommendation for increased intake in TASF, partly due to the relatively high prevalence of undernutrition and micronutrient deficiencies in these regions ([15](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r15)). A systematic review of the literature about the relative health impacts of diets with reduced GHG emissions revealed that across all indicators of “healthiness”, 64% of lower GHG emission diets were linked to worse nutritional and health indicators ([16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r16)). Reduced saturated fat and salt were often associated with diets low in animal products, but these diets were often also high in sugar and low in essential micronutrients.

When considering the timeline from 2020 to 2050, the estimated 20% increase in demand for TASF can be met by a proportionate rise in animal numbers, a stable or preferably decreasing number of more productive animals, alternative protein products, dietary changes, reduced waste, or more pragmatically some combination of all of these. In 2023, the FAO released a report ([5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r5)) outlining the most promising interventions to cumulatively reduce projected GHG resulting from TASF demand by 55% though 2050, relative to a “business as usual” no mitigation scenario. These included improvements in animal and feed management, including productivity increases (20%), improved breeding (15%) and animal health (10%), adoption of known feed and nutrition practices (5%), and rumen manipulation with CH 4 inhibitors (5%) ([Fig. 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#fig01)). This report also suggested that dietary shift had a limited reduction potential based on the fact that in LMIC, the typical diet often has low GHG because it falls below recommended calorie levels and lacks sufficient proteins, fruits, vegetables, and nuts. In those regions, a shift towards the recommended FBDG would be generally associated with increased overall consumption and a higher quantity of both plant- and animal-based foods ([12](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r12)). Given that population growth, and the need for increased dietary levels of TASF is occurring in LMIC countries, and that these countries are home to 76% of the global cattle herd and contribute 75% of the global ruminant GHG emissions ([17](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r17)), this would seem to be the logical place to focus emission reduction efforts. Increasing livestock productivity generally requires simultaneous interventions in the areas of animal feed, health, and genetics ([18](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r18)).

Fig. 1.
-------

[![Image 9: Fig. 1.](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/9a59/11648654/ef5979d69a37/pnas.2319001121fig01.jpg)](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/core/lw/2.0/html/tileshop_pmc/tileshop_pmc_inline.html?title=Click%20on%20image%20to%20zoom&p=PMC3&id=11648654_pnas.2319001121fig01.jpg)

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/figure/fig01/)

Base year and projected emissions from livestock systems shown as a waterfall chart with a range of mitigation measures applied to 2050 with their technical potential. Image comes from FAO. 2023. Pathways towards lower emissions – A global assessment of the GHG emissions and mitigation options from livestock agrifood systems. Rome [https://doi.org/10.4060/cc9029en](https://doi.org/10.4060/cc9029en) and is published under the CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 IGO license.

In 2021, the global cattle (152.9 billion) and buffalo (203.9 million) populations collectively produced 74.9 Mt meat and 757 and 149 Mt of milk, respectively ([19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r19)). The majority (59%) of beef is produced in mixed crop and livestock systems, as compared to only 7% (2% global cattle population) in intensive feedlot systems, with the remaining 34% being produced in grazing systems ([20](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r20)). This latter group can be further divided, first into intensive grazing systems that are found in tropical and temperate zones where high-quality grasslands and fodder production can support larger numbers of highly productive animals (e.g., Australia). These systems are mostly focused on food production, based on individual landownership, and supply about 20% of global beef production. The second category are pastoral livestock grazing systems that have developed in harsh environments, such as dry lands and cold areas, and which account for less than 15% of total beef production, but which support the livelihoods of 200 to 500 million pastoralists ([21](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r21)). Mixed crop–livestock systems are responsible for 58% of total emissions, whereas grazing-based systems contribute 19% ([22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r22)). Industrial and other systems comprise the remaining 23%.

The intake of ruminants in grazing and mixed systems is mainly composed (about 90%) of non-human edible roughage: leaves, grass, silage, and crop residues ([23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r23)). Ruminants need 0.6 kg of human-edible protein feed intake to produce 1 kg of protein (as compared to the 2 kg requirement of monogastrics), which makes them net contributors to global human-edible protein production. The FAO estimates permanent meadows and pastures comprise 67% of the 4.8 billion hectares that are used for agriculture globally. Of the 3.5 billion hectares of permanent grasslands globally, 1.5 billion are not suitable for grazing, and 2 billion hectares are grazed by ruminants. Of these, 1.3 billion hectares of pastures and rangelands are considered marginal, meaning they are not suitable to be converted to croplands or forests due to climatic, soil fertility, and topographical factors ([23](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r23)). Ruminants are uniquely positioned to convert this cellulosic biomass into nutrient-dense TASF. This is part of ruminants’ role in circular food systems ([24](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r24)). In their absence, this land would produce no human food.

Where and how cattle are raised has important implications on the emissions intensity of production. Innovations in the three main disciplines of animal science, genetics, nutrition, and health, historically supported by extension programs that facilitate adoption of best practices, have been associated both with efficiency gains and large decreases in the emissions intensities (emissions produced per unit of product) in high-income countries ([25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r25)). However, this improvement has not been universal, and many LMIC have production systems with high emissions intensities ([26](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r26)). The oft-cited global average of 99.5 kg CO 2 eq/kg beef ([3](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r3)) hides considerable regional variation. Emissions intensity can vary 50-fold among producers of the same product in similar geographic regions. Chang et al. ([27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r27)) estimated that improving livestock production efficiencies in the 10 countries with the largest emission reduction potential (Madagascar, Morocco, Niger, South Africa, Tanzania), Asia (China, India, Iran, Turkey), and South America (Brazil) could contribute 60% to 65% of the global reduction in livestock emissions by 2050 (compared to a baseline where emissions intensities are held constant in the future). These authors reported that efforts to improve production efficiencies would have a much greater potential for GHG mitigation than demand-side efforts to promote balanced, healthy, and environmentally sustainable diets. These efforts alone would not be sufficient to mitigate TASF emissions.

It is worth noting that the production of beef is not proportional to the size of the cattle population. The United States produced 18% (12.7 Mt) of the world’s beef with 6% (93.8 million) of the world’s cattle in 2021. Brazil produced 13% (9.75 Mt) of the word’s beef with 15% (225 million) of the world’s cattle population, whereas India with 193 million cattle and 112 million buffalo produced only 5.8% (4.20 Mt) of bovid meat. Of particular importance is the African continent population, which produced around 9.5% of the world’s beef with almost 25% (373 million) of the world’s cattle ([19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r19)). There are a myriad of reasons why beef production is not the main driver of cattle numbers, including the fact cattle are also raised for milk and other coproducts like hides.

Most carbon footprint assessment studies focus only on edible products and do not account for the multifunctionality of livestock. In many regions, particularly in LMIC, livestock are not only raised for food production but also for their role in providing draft power, serving as a financial asset and as a means of saving. Additionally, cattle in India have religious significance which forbids the consumption of beef, and in Africa, manure production and animal traction are key functions served by cattle for crop production in mixed systems ([28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r28)). In other words, cattle are not raised solely, or even primarily, for beef in many countries, and hence, beef production efficiency is not the main objective of these farmers. Evaluating these systems based on metrics that value edible products alone ignores the multifaceted roles that ruminants play in global agri-food systems. Some have suggested that GHG emissions in such systems should be allocated across the multiple functions to appropriately acknowledge nonsalable products and other roles that cattle play in smallholder systems ([29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r29)). These authors argue that using a traditional single-issue LCA (i.e., kg CO 2 eq) in “complex mixed smallholder dairy systems with multifunctional aspects related to cattle keeping” does not “grasp the system for what it is. It follows that policy recommendations stemming from such a fundamental misapprehension of smallholder agricultural practice will be misguided, at best”, and that a livelihoods lens is needed to define “productivity” ([29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r29)).

Moreover, beef is actually not even half of the picture with respect to cattle numbers, because milk constitutes 67% of the total protein produced by cattle. India produces 24% (214 Mt) of the world’s buffalo and cattle milk, followed by the United States 11.3% (103 MT), Pakistan 6.5% (59 Mt), China 4.4% (40 Mt), and Brazil 3.9% (36 Mt). About half of India’s milk production is from water buffalo, and the other half is from cattle. The European Union as a collective produces around 25.1% (227 Mt) of the world’s milk with 5% (76 million) of the world’s cattle population and Africa as a collective 4.7% (42 Mt) of milk ([19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r19)). World milk production is projected to grow at 1.5% p.a. over the next decade to 1 085 Mt in 2033 ([30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r30)), faster than most other main agricultural commodities. Over half of the increase in total milk production is anticipated to come from India and Pakistan, which will jointly account for over 32% of world production by 2032. According to an OECD report ([30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r30)), “the global level of GHG emissions will largely depend on efficiency gains in India and other countries with high cattle populations and extensive production.”

Opportunities in Breeding and Genetic Improvement
-------------------------------------------------

The FAO report ([5](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r5)) detailed a number of promising interventions (improved livestock diets, genetics, veterinary care, and management practices) to reduce projected GHG resulting from TASF demand by 55% though 2050. It should be noted that this report assumes a full adoption of intervention, illustrating what is possible (technical mitigation potential), rather than the extent to which these practices will be adopted (economic mitigation potential) ([25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r25)). As a geneticist, I am going to focus on opportunities in breeding as a particularly attractive approach to productivity enhancements, as following implementation genetic improvements are permanent and cumulative ([31](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r31)). There are some relatively simple technologies, such as altering the sex ratio of offspring, and using artificial insemination (AI) to increase the use of genetically superior bulls ([32](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r32)), that can have major impacts on the efficiency and carbon footprint of cattle production systems. First introduced in the early 1940s, AI has been widely adopted in the dairy industry of many high-income countries, helping to achieve a more than two-fold decrease in the GHG per kg of milk in the United States from 3.66 in 1944 to 1.35 in 2007 ([33](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r33)). However, the adoption of AI has not been universal and is particularly low in LMIC and extensive beef production systems where it is difficult to coordinate estrus synchronization protocols and perform AI ([34](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r34)). The following section details efforts to increase AI adoption in Brazil and India, the two individual countries with the largest global cattle populations. Additionally, in high-income countries, the recent adoption of genomic testing and sexed semen has resulted in a dramatic change in the origin of calves entering the beef supply chain ([35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r35)), with further sustainability implications for cattle production in those countries.

Lessons from India and Brazil
-----------------------------

By 2050, India’s human population is projected to reach 1.65 billion which represents a 16% increase from the current figure of 1.43 billion people, of which 8.8% currently rely on the livestock sector for their employment, and 20.5 million individuals for their livelihoods ([36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r36)). Milk yield per cow was only 424 kg/y in 1961, limited by a shortage of quality feeds and fodders and few high-yielding milk cows. However, yield increased more than four-fold to 1777 kg per animal in 2019–2020, still shy of the global average of 2,699 kg. Part of this was due to substantial government investment, in partnership with the World Bank, in Operation Flood from 1970 to 1996. In 30 y, it transformed India from a milk-deficient nation into the largest milk-producing country in the world. This growth in the dairy sector was both pro-poor and pro-women, having multiple beneficial effects including nutrition, education (especially of girls), and job-creation, and is helping India to make progress toward sustainable development goals (SDGs) addressing poverty and promoting economic growth ([36](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r36)). Milk consumption by children aged 6 to 59 mo in India, a country with 24% of undernourished children worldwide, has been associated with reduced odds of stunting, underweight, and anthropometric failure ([37](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r37)). The World bank calculated that for an initial investment of 2 billion rupees in Operation Flood II, the net return/year to rural economy was 240 billion rupees ([38](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r38)). This is a very high input–output ratio for a development program. According to the World Bank, this was due in part to the fact that this project “encouraged members to invest in biological assets—milk cows—that periodically reproduce themselves without major reinvestment and continue to yield regular benefits, utilizing crop residues that otherwise do not have much economic value” ([39](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r39)).

This increased milk yield per cow was partly a result of improved genetics enabled by the use of AI starting in 1985 and which continues to be the backbone of bovid breeding programs in India ([40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r40)). The total number of AI performed in India increased five-fold this century from 19.77 million in 2000–2001 to 98 million in 2021–2022. Even so, the overall AI coverage rate in bovines in India remains relatively low at ~30% with a conception rate of 35% ([40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r40)), and AI coverage rates on buffalo in India and Pakistan are 35% and 14%, respectively, suggesting considerable room for improvement given the right production incentives, investment, and institutional support. The percentage of crossbred dairy cows rose from 17% in 1990–91 to 38.3% in 2021–22, with their contribution to total cow milk production increasing from 33.5% to 61.2% ([41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11648654/#r41)). Concurrently, there has been a noticeable trend toward raising more female cattle. The number of female cows climbed from 102.98 million in 1992 to 145.12 million in 2017, while the number of male cattle fell from 101.59 million to 47.4 million. Since 1997, the country's milk production has increased an additional 140 Mt (306%), driven mostly by improved productivity per cow in combination with a comparatively modest 6% increase (~ 16.67 million head) in the overall bovid population, characterized by a decline of 5.28 million cattle and an increase of 21.9 milli

enriched_points.point_title: Rising Global Demand for Animal Products
enriched_points.expanded_content: The global animal produce market is projected to grow from USD 1.6 trillion in 2023 to USD 2.3 trillion by 2032, driven by increasing protein consumption.
As awareness of environmental impacts rises, sustainable farming practices, including eco-friendly feed and humane treatment, are becoming essential to meet consumer demands.

enriched_points.point_title: Role of Feed Efficiency in Meeting Demands
enriched_points.expanded_content: Optimizing feed efficiency reduces resource waste and greenhouse gas emissions, providing a viable solution to meet the growing global demand for animal products sustainably.
